text: The surgeon attempts to reduce the colon from a right scrotal hernia, revealing numerous attachments that hinder full reduction. A peritoneal flap is initiated, with a Cartier instrument placed in the left lower quadrant to aid in retraction. The third instrument enhances the use of the left hand for assisting the right hand during dissection, mimicking open surgery techniques.

The surgeon develops the peritoneal flap in the space of Retzius using blunt dissection, cauterizing small vessels as needed. The dissection progresses laterally, staying close to the peritoneum to avoid leaving retro-rectus fat against the rectus muscle. The third instrument aids in maintaining the ideal plane by retracting tissue, while lobular fat remains against the abdominal wall. Blood from a previous tap block is visible.

The surgeon identifies multiple layers to separate from the peritoneum, revealing the cecum as a retroperitoneal structure sliding into the indirect space. Without a peritoneal sac posteriorly, the surgeon carefully dissects close to the cecum, using small blood vessels as a guide for the dissection.

The surgeon uses the third arm to assist in precise dissection of microfacial layers, removing small blood vessels to separate the testicular vessels from the hernia sac. The testicular vessels are kept against the retroperitoneum to avoid injury during dissection into the scrotum or inguinal canal. The surgeon connects the space of Bargros dissection to the space of Retsius, taking care around the VAS deferens, which is positioned more posteriorly than expected.

The surgeon uses a third instrument to assist in precise dissection at the tip of the hernia, avoiding bleeding and ensuring coordination between the left and right hands. A scissor is used to gently push and identify tissue planes, observing subtle movements to determine which layers can be divided. The surgeon navigates through scar tissue in the medial area of the hernia, finding an areolar plane to separate the hernia contents from the testicular vessels, which are identified later in the dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects a fascial layer, ensuring the testicular vessel remains undisturbed and separate from the hernia sac. The hernia is mostly reduced, but the surgeon continues to search for the tip of the hernia sac. The medial dissection is reserved for last due to its complexity, with the external iliac vein becoming more visible as the dissection progresses posteriorly.

The surgeon identifies and reduces a cord lipoma or fibro-fatty connective tissue from the hernia sac, located anterior to the iliopubic tract. Careful dissection is performed to avoid injuring the genital and femoral branches of the genital femoral nerve. The surgeon uses a third instrument for retraction, revealing the tip of the hernia sac and ensuring no critical structures like the vas deferens or testicular vessels are injured during tissue division.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the hernia sac, removing excess fat while maintaining awareness of the potential presence of testicular vessels within the tissue. The vas deferens is located amidst dense scar tissue, and the surgeon proceeds cautiously to separate the scar and medial attachments of the hernia sac. The vas deferens is eventually revealed, confirming it was not at risk during the dissection.

The surgeon removes a clump of fat from the hernia sac, ensuring it is managed separately from the sac itself. The hernia sac is completely reduced, and the surgeon proceeds to parietalize the cord, ensuring good posterior coverage by dissecting the peritoneum off the posterior structures. Pink bladder tissue is visible, indicating a transition in tissue layers. The surgeon dissects across the midline and about two centimeters deep to Cooper's ligament to ensure the mesh will lay flat in the retropubic space, removing peritoneum and bladder tissue posteriorly to allow mesh transition from the retropubic space to the lateral pelvic space.

The surgeon identifies the obturator fat plug at the posterior extent of the dissection, ensuring ample space around the indirect defect. The surgeon points out the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve and attempts to locate the general branch, which is difficult to see due to its small size. Extra fatty connective tissue or lipoma is cleaned up and moved away to allow the mesh to lay flat against the pelvic sidewall.

The surgeon identifies the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, ensuring it remains protected by a fascial layer to prevent injury. A 16x12 right-sided anatomical ProGrip mesh is positioned with the seam approximately two centimeters behind the posterior edge of the internal ring and aligned with Cooper's ligament. The mesh is adjusted to conform to the pelvic curvature, ensuring proper coverage from left to right and preventing displacement when the bladder moves.

The surgeon encounters a large mass involving the colon, appendix, and terminal ileum within a sac-like structure. Attempts are made to explore and potentially free the colon by cutting bands, but the retroperitoneal structure proves difficult to manipulate. A 3-0 V-lock suture is used, and a third instrument assists in elevating the peritoneum to prevent tearing while managing the heavy colon.
